text: The video shows the technique of sphincteroplasty. The surgeon marks a semicircular incision at the periphery of the pigmented skin near the external sphincter. Local anesthetic is infused, and the skin is incised along the marked line.

A self-retaining Lone Star retractor is positioned to expose the surgical field. The surgeon explores the space between the reflections of Collie's fascia of the anal canal and the vagina, carefully separating the rectocele from the vaginal wall to avoid breaching either structure.

The space between the anal canal and vagina is packed with a swab. The surgeon mobilizes the left half of the external sphincter, followed by the right half. Further mobilization extends into the left ischiorectal fat, and the transversus perinei muscle is also mobilized. The external sphincter is palpated to confirm anatomical landmarks, with care taken to avoid damaging the pudendal nerve and vessels in the ischiorectal fossa.
The surgeon placates the rectocele using PDS sutures. Following this, an overlap repair of the external sphincter is performed, indicating the completion of the mobilization process.

The surgeon repairs the mobilized transversus perianae muscle. The self-retaining retractor is removed, and a surgical drain is inserted. The posterior fornix of the vagina is repaired to ensure the maintenance of sexual function.
The surgeon closes the incision and examines the sphincter to ensure it remains intact. The result is visually evident as a longer anogenital distance and improved pelvic floor strength.
